generic:
The Riverside Park Amphitheater is one of the most popular outdoor music venues in the region, hosting concerts, festivals, and cultural events throughout the summer season. The venue is managed by BrightStage Events, which oversees everything from scheduling performers to coordinating food vendors and managing security. Every year, BrightStage works with dozens of touring musicians, promoters, and artist managers to arrange appearances at Riverside Park.  

This summer, the rising indie-rock group Midnight Echo has signed on to headline a major weekend festival at Riverside Park. Their tour manager, Riley Voss, is handling the negotiations with BrightStage. The festival is expected to bring in tens of thousands of attendees, and a successful weekend could help both the band and the venue build their reputations further.  

Although the performance agreement between Midnight Echo and BrightStage has already been signed, Riley and the BrightStage venue manager, Jordan Hale, still need to negotiate an important matter: backstage accommodations. The Riverside Park Amphitheater has limited high-quality backstage amenities, including premium dressing rooms attached directly to the stage, as well as additional but less convenient modular trailers set up nearby.  
Because multiple artists will be performing over the course of the festival, the backstage facilities must be divided fairly among the different groups. At Riverside, there are 15 premium dressing rooms with private bathrooms and direct stage access, and 45 modular trailers located a short walk behind the stage area. While both options provide the essentials, artists strongly prefer the premium rooms. The trailers, though functional, are less comfortable and lack the prestige of the premium areas.  

Over the past few years, BrightStage has received consistent complaints from artist managers that rivals were offered better backstage accommodations. In response, BrightStage created a system to allocate these fairly. The total number of dressing spaces a band receives is based on its group size and stage time. Within that total, BrightStage tries to balance the distribution of premium versus trailer spaces. Festivals are high-profile, and competition for premium rooms is fierce.  

This system, however, also created new tensions. Tour managers want as many premium rooms and as few trailers as possible. BrightStage, on the other hand, needs to ensure each band gets a reasonable share of trailers, reserving premium rooms for the most established artists. Riley Voss and Jordan Hale now face this challenge in their backstage negotiation.  
Last year, Midnight Echo had a tense conversation with BrightStage regarding dressing room allocations. After lengthy discussion, Midnight Echo was given 12 total backstage spaces—10 trailers and only 2 premium rooms. Riley Voss was deeply dissatisfied, feeling the band had been treated unfairly compared to other performers with similar billing who received more premium spaces for fewer total rooms. Riley complained formally that BrightStage seemed to favor older acts or those from larger management companies. Jordan Hale, the festival grounds manager, defended the allocation as reasonable, pointing out that Midnight Echo was still a relatively new headliner and that larger, established names were entitled to a bigger share of premium dressing rooms.  

This year, tensions are likely to run high again. Midnight Echo’s popularity has skyrocketed, and Riley expects that the band should receive significantly more premium accommodations to reflect both their elevated status and the demands of their growing entourage. Meanwhile, Jordan must balance requests from several major acts on the same weekend lineup, each of whom insists on premium rooms for their headliners. Negotiations will center on how many total backstage spaces Midnight Echo will lease for the festival, and—most importantly—how many of those will be premium rooms.
agent1_specific:
As the Festival Grounds Manager for Riverside Park, you are eager for Midnight Echo to lease a large number of backstage trailer spaces this year. Demand for backstage facilities is high, and the premium dressing rooms are under heavy pressure from several legacy acts who have been booked for the same festival weekend. These larger, veteran artists have negotiated preferential treatment by insisting on more premium dressing rooms as part of their contracts, and BrightStage has already promised them additional access. Because of these commitments, you need to be careful not to give away too many premium rooms to new headliners like Midnight Echo, no matter how quickly their reputation is growing.  

At the same time, BrightStage has recently invested in upgrading the modular trailers with air conditioning units, small lounge areas, and improved power hookups, but many bands still view them as "second-class" accommodations. You have a surplus of trailers this year and need to ensure they are filled; otherwise, BrightStage risks losing revenue while expensive premium spaces remain over-subscribed. From your perspective, it would be ideal for Midnight Echo to commit to leasing significantly more trailers, while keeping their premium allocation low.  
You are not looking forward to the upcoming meeting with Riley Voss. Even though Midnight Echo is not yet one of the most established or highest-grossing acts on the festival circuit, Riley behaves as though the band deserves top-tier treatment across all areas of negotiations. In your past interactions, Riley has been difficult—quick to argue about minor logistical concerns such as tent placement, stage sound checks, and catering requests, often demanding a level of attention that BrightStage simply cannot provide to every band. You have struggled to remain patient, especially when Riley becomes confrontational about fairness and favoritism.  

Backstage allocation has been the toughest issue with Midnight Echo in particular. In your judgment, you offered the band a reasonable set of spaces last year, consistent with their group size and billing. However, Riley constantly accused you of bias, claiming that other artists were receiving better premium-to-trailer ratios despite having fewer entourage demands. To some extent, that criticism had merit—you know you have rewarded long-standing artists with more premium access—but that is part of BrightStage’s strategy to maintain loyalty from acts that consistently draw the largest audiences.  
To reduce recurring complaints and to maintain some sense of order this year, BrightStage has set a new guideline: **no band may receive more than one third of its total backstage spaces as premium rooms.** You believe this limit will help distribute the most coveted rooms more fairly across multiple performers while also encouraging touring managers to increase their overall number of leased spaces if they want more premium access. The rule is designed to give you leverage in negotiations, but in practice, you would still prefer to allocate **less than one third premium** to Midnight Echo in order to preserve your limited stock of high-demand rooms for other headline acts.  

Since Midnight Echo has expanded its touring crew and entourage considerably, you anticipate Riley will push for both more overall spaces and a higher proportion of premium rooms. Your strategy will be to steer Riley into accepting more trailers as proof of the band’s growth, while limiting the number of premium allocations to as few as possible without jeopardizing the deal.  

Ideally, you want to increase their total leased count above last year’s 12, encouraging them to take more trailers.  
To help prepare yourself for the negotiation, you have developed a scoring system, along with a minimum acceptable score threshold for BrightStage. Here is how it works:  

- For **each backstage space above 12 total** (whether trailer or premium) that Midnight Echo leases, **ADD 5 points** to your score.  
- For **each premium dressing room above 3**, **SUBTRACT 3 points** from your score.  

This scoring system reflects BrightStage’s priorities—expanding overall rentals (especially filling trailers) while protecting your limited premium inventory. Your calculations show that even if Midnight Echo decided not to lease any backstage spaces at all, you could still fill them with other touring artists, though at a competitive disadvantage. You have estimated that your **BATNA (best alternative to a negotiated agreement)** is worth 45 points—the score you could secure by reallocating space to other performers. Thus, you cannot accept any agreement with Riley worth fewer than 45 points.  

Here’s an example of how you calculate your total: If Midnight Echo agrees to lease 24 backstage spaces, with 5 of those being premium rooms, your score is 54. (Spaces above 12: 12 × 5 = +60 points; premium rooms above 3: 2 × 3 = −6 points; total = 54.)
agent2_specific:
You are preparing to negotiate with Jordan Hale about backstage accommodations for the festival, and you are already dreading the conversation. Last year was a frustrating struggle in which you walked away with far fewer premium dressing rooms than you believed Midnight Echo deserved. Your band members and support crew were crammed into trailers that felt cramped, noisy, and poorly ventilated. Worse than the conditions themselves was the humiliation: members of other acts, with smaller followings and less prominence, waltzed into their private, premium rooms while your group trudged across the gravel lots to reach modest modular units. That episode created unnecessary tension within the band, and you bore the brunt of complaints from your artists and their assistants.  

This year, the stakes are even higher. Midnight Echo’s reputation has grown considerably, and fans already see them as the rightful headliner of the entire weekend. Your regional press contacts have emphasized how crucial optics are for a band on the rise. The band expects elevated treatment backstage as proof of their newfound stature. In particular, the core members are adamant that they cannot tolerate another set of negotiations ending in mostly trailer assignments.  
You know Jordan Hale, the festival grounds manager, is a tough negotiator who is constantly under pressure to please multiple stakeholders. From past experience, you believe Jordan favors established legacy acts and management companies with whom BrightStage has long-term relationships. This frustrates you, since you see how unfairly newer, fast-rising groups are often treated. Your band puts in the work to draw in huge crowds, yet they still get pushed into inferior arrangements. Jordan has also been dismissive with you during past negotiations, being slow to respond to requests and brushing off your concerns, which makes it difficult to trust their assurances about “fairness.”  

Your budget this year is also tighter than you’d like, since much of the money has already been committed to transportation, crew salaries, and stage equipment. You cannot simply agree to lease a large number of trailers in order to boost your premium room allocation. Realistically, you would prefer to keep the total dressing spaces close to the current count, around 12, though you could stretch as high as 20 if necessary.  
What you absolutely must secure in this negotiation is a significant increase in premium dressing rooms compared to last year’s deal. You have calculated that if you return to the band with fewer than six premium rooms, you will face backlash from the group’s core members and their management executives. They are already vocal about the need for comfort and status backstage, especially since industry press representatives and VIP guests will likely tour the behind-the-scenes areas. If journalists see the band squeezed into trailers while other acts enjoy premium accommodations, it would undermine Midnight Echo’s growing reputation.  

Your ideal scenario would be to secure at least eight premium rooms, which would be enough for the four core band members and their immediate personal assistants. This would also allow some flexibility for key crew leaders, such as the production manager and tour photographer, to be housed in premium conditions. You are prepared to argue that providing more premium spaces is not just a matter of convenience, but an investment in the festival’s reputation. If BrightStage treats Midnight Echo as a headliner with appropriate accommodations, the story will reflect positively on the festival itself. Failure to provide this treatment will feel like BrightStage is stifling one of its biggest draws.  
To help you negotiate strategically with Jordan, you have created a scoring system and a minimum acceptable score that reflects both budgetary limitations and your band’s non‑negotiable demand for premium spaces. For each dressing space above 12 (whether premium or trailer), you SUBTRACT 1 point from your score, since every additional space increases rental costs and stretches your resources. However, for each premium dressing room secured, you ADD 5 points, because those directly satisfy both the band’s comfort and publicity objectives. You consider last year’s outcome (12 spaces in total, with 2 premium rooms and 10 trailers) highly inadequate—it scores only 10 points under this system—and it provides a baseline for comparison.

Based on these calculations, you estimate that your BATNA—renting temporary portable trailers through a third‑party vendor, which would be significantly less convenient and outside the secured backstage area—is worth about 20 points. This option includes some service staff but lacks the prestige and visibility of the official festival facilities. Therefore, you may not accept an agreement with BrightStage that is worth fewer than 20 points.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did Jordan and Riley reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if Jordan and Riley did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.

3. Based on the following conversation, if Jordan and Riley reached an agreement on the total number of backstage dressing spaces, on what total number of spaces? Answer with a number or N/A if they did not reach an agreement.

4. Based on the following conversation, if Jordan and Riley reached an agreement, on what number of backstage spaces in the premium dressing rooms? Answer with a number or N/A if they did not reach an agreement.
5. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for Jordan Hale? Answer with a number.
6. Based on the following conversation, what was the point score for Riley Voss? Answer with a number.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         how many total backstage spaces did Midnight Echo agree to lease?          Answer with a number between 0-60 or N/A, without any other text."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and min(1.0, max(0.0, (float(m.group()) - 12) * 5 / 240))     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         how many premium dressing rooms did Midnight Echo agree to lease?          Answer with a number between 0-15 or N/A, without any other text."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+", val))         and max(0.0, 1.0 - max(0.0, float(m.group()) - 3) * 3 / 36)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did the final agreement keep the proportion of premium rooms at or below one third of total backstage spaces?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     )) and re.search(r"\bYES\b", val, flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,           how many premium dressing rooms did Midnight Echo receive?           Answer with a number between 0-15 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and min(1.0, float(m.group()) / 15)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         how many total backstage spaces did Midnight Echo receive?          Answer with a number between 0-60 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0.0, 1.0 - max(0.0, float(m.group()) - 12) / 8)     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Midnight Echo receive at least 6 premium dressing rooms?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Midnight Echo receive at least 8 premium dressing rooms?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Midnight Echo secure more premium rooms than last year (more than 2)?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Festival Grounds Manager and Band Tour Manager reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview:

learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants recognize the importance of probing underlying interests rather than just stated positions, and to understand how grounding proposals in fair, external standards can strengthen both the legitimacy and durability of agreements. Learners will also examine how the level of ambition they set going into a negotiation directly influences the quality of the outcomes they achieve. Additionally, they will practice identifying issues that may hold different levels of importance to each side and learn how exchanging concessions across these issues can generate greater joint gains. Finally, the case highlights the inherent balance between collaborating to expand the overall value available and competing to secure a favorable share for oneself, encouraging students to navigate the interplay between cooperation and competition.
title: Backstage Battle at Riverside Park
oneline: A negotiation between Midnight Echo’s tour manager and the Riverside Park festival manager over how many total backstage spaces the band will lease and how many of them will be premium rooms.
